title: Apply: regional climates
description: Apply your understanding of regional climates in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS

assessment_items.question_content:
Eureka and Redding are two cities located in California. During the summer months, Eureka has much lower average temperatures than Redding does. Geographic data that can help explain this difference in climate is shown in the table below.

City | Proximity to water | Latitude | Elevation
:-: | :-:  | :-: | :-:
Eureka | Coastal | $40.8\degree\,\text{N}$ | $12\,\text{m}$
Redding | Inland | $40.6\degree\,\text{N}$ | $172\,\text{m}$

**Which geographic factor is most responsible for the summer climate difference between Eureka and Redding?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Fresno and Monterey are two cities located in California. During the summer months, Fresno has much higher average temperatures than Monterey does. Geographic data that can help explain this difference in climate is shown in the table below.

City | Latitude | Elevation | Distance from water
:-: | :-:  | :-: | :-:
Fresno | $36.7\degree\,\text{N}$ | $94\,\text{m}$ | Inland
Monterey | $36.6\degree\,\text{N}$ | $8\,\text{m}$ | Coastal

**Which geographic factor is most responsible for the summer climate difference between Fresno and Monterey?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Bakersfield and San Diego are two cities located in California. During the summer months, Bakersfield has much higher average temperatures than San Diego does. Geographic data that can help explain this difference in climate is shown in the table below.

City | Distance from water | Latitude | Elevation
:-: | :-:  | :-: | :-:
Bakersfield | Inland | $35.4\degree\,\text{N}$ | $123\,\text{m}$
San Diego | Coastal | $32.7\degree\,\text{N}$ | $19\,\text{m}$

**Which geographic factor is most responsible for the summer climate difference between Bakersfield and San Diego?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The graph below shows the average monthly temperatures for three cities in Arizona. All of the cities are located at roughly the same latitude. However, each city is located at a different elevation.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on this temperature data, which of the three cities is located at the lowest elevation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that places at lower elevations generally have *higher* average temperatures.
According to the graph, the city with the highest average temperature each month is Yuma.
So, of the three cities, Yuma is located at the lowest elevation.

options.alt: A line graph is shown. The y-axis is labeled Average temperature (°C) and goes from –5 to 40. The x-axis is labeled Month and goes from January to December. The graph contains 3 curves labeled Yuma, Kingman, and Flagstaff. Each curve follows a similar path, rising from January to July or August and falling from July or August to December. The curve for Yuma is higher than the curve for Kingman, and the curve for Kingman is higher than the curve for Flagstaff.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the average monthly temperatures for three cities in Arizona. All of the cities are located at roughly the same latitude. However, each city is located at a different elevation.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on this temperature data, which of the three cities is located at the highest elevation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that places at higher elevations generally have *lower* average temperatures.
According to the graph, the city with the lowest average temperature each month is Flagstaff.
So, of the three cities, Flagstaff is located at the highest elevation.

options.alt: A line graph is shown. The y-axis is labeled Average temperature (°C) and goes from –5 to 40. The x-axis is labeled Month and goes from January to December. The graph contains 3 curves labeled Bullhead City, Sedona, and Flagstaff. Each curve follows a similar path, rising from January to July and falling from July to December. The curve for Bullhead City is higher than the curve for Sedona, and the curve for Sedona is higher than the curve for Flagstaff.

assessment_items.question_content:
The map below represents a portion of an imaginary continent on Earth. Two coastal locations on the continent are labeled A and B.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which location is likely to have the cooler climate, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that places generally have a cooler climate when they receive *less* direct sunlight throughout the year.
A place generally receives less direct sunlight when it is at a *higher* latitude (farther from the equator).
According to the map, location A is at a higher latitude than location B.
So, location A is likely to have the cooler climate, and this is because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year.

options.alt: A map is shown. Three latitudes are indicated on the map, labeled from bottom to top as 0° (equator), 30° N, and 60° N. Location A is above 60° N. Location B is between the equator and 30° N.

choices.content: Location A, because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year
Location A, because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year
Location B, because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year
Location B, because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year

assessment_items.question_content:
The map below represents a portion of an imaginary continent on Earth. Two coastal locations on the continent are labeled A and B.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which location is likely to have a warmer climate, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that places generally have a warmer climate when they receive *more* direct sunlight throughout the year.
A place generally receives more direct sunlight when it is at a *lower* latitude (closer to the equator).
According to the map, location B is at a lower latitude than location A.
So, location B is likely to have the warmer climate, and this is because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year.

options.alt: A map is shown. Three latitudes are indicated on the map, labeled from bottom to top as 0° (equator), 30° N, and 60° N. Location A is above 60° N. Location B is between the equator and 30° N.

choices.content: Location A, because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year
Location A, because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year
Location B, because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year
Location B, because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year

options.alt: A map is shown. Three latitudes are indicated on the map, labeled from top to bottom as 0° (equator), 30° S, and 60° S. Location A is between the equator and 30° S. Location B is below 60° S.

choices.content: Location A, because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year
Location A, because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year
Location B, because it receives less direct sunlight throughout the year
Location B, because it receives more direct sunlight throughout the year

assessment_items.question_content:
The cross section below shows the formation of a rain shadow behind a mountain. Two cities, labeled A and B, are located on either side of the mountain.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How does the climate of city A compare to that of city B?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To start, let’s figure out where the rain shadow is in the diagram. Remember that a rain shadow forms on the side of a mountain that faces *away* from the direction of the wind.
According to the diagram, the wind is moving from left to right over the mountain. So, the rain shadow must be on the *right* side of the mountain.

This means that city A is located outside of the rain shadow, and city B is located inside of the rain shadow. In general, the climate outside of a rain shadow is *cooler* and *wetter* than the climate inside of a rain shadow.
So, the climate of city A is cooler and wetter than that of city B.

options.alt: A cross-section shows an ocean on the left and a mountain on the right. City A is located on the left side of the mountain. City B is located on the right side of the mountain at the same elevation. Arrows indicate that wind originates over the ocean and then flows from left to right over the mountain.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cross section below shows the formation of a rain shadow behind a mountain. Two cities, labeled A and B, are located on either side of the mountain.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How does the climate of city B compare to that of city A?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To start, let’s figure out where the rain shadow is in the diagram. Remember that a rain shadow forms on the side of a mountain that faces *away* from the direction of the wind.
According to the diagram, the wind is moving from left to right over the mountain. So, the rain shadow must be on the *right* side of the mountain.

This means that city B is located inside of the rain shadow, and city A is located outside of the rain shadow. In general, the climate inside of a rain shadow is *warmer* and *drier* than the climate outside of a rain shadow.
So, the climate of city B is warmer and drier than that of city A.

City B is located on the right side of the mountain at the same elevation. Arrows indicate that wind originates over the ocean and then flows from left to right over the mountain.

According to the diagram, the wind is moving from left to right over the mountain. So, the rain shadow must be on the *right* side of the mountain.
